title: Florida pastor sues OpenAI after ChatGPT dismissed his symptoms for six weeks before a near-fatal pulmonary embolism
excerpt: Scott Winters suffered from blood clots in both lungs after ChatGPT told him to "take it easy.” Now, he’s taking legal action.
content_html:
Godwin Oluponmile

Fri, July 24, 2026 at 8:30 AM CDT 7 min read

Scott Winters was mid-sermon on June 8, 2025 when the room began to spin — badly enough that he had to stop the service. Church staff called paramedics, who examined him and said he appeared stable.

When he got back home, he opened ChatGPT to explain the episode. ChatGPT told him to "take it easy" and said his symptoms would improve over time, according to the complaint filed on his behalf July 22 in San Francisco County Superior Court.

Must Read

  • Jeff Bezos backs a platform that lets anyone invest in rental homes for as little as $100 — 6 ways to build wealth like a landlord without actually being one

  • Dave Ramsey warns nearly 50% of Americans are making 1 big Social Security mistake — here's what it is and 3 simple steps to fix it ASAP

  • Millionaires under 43 hold only 25% of their wealth in stocks. Here's where their money is actually going

Unfortunately, on July 13, 2025, he was rushed to an intensive care unit with blood clots in both lungs.

Winters, 55, a former pastor and real estate professional who lives in Florida, is suing OpenAI and CEO Sam Altman on eight causes of action, including strict product liability, negligence and violations of California's Unfair Competition Law. Tech Justice Law, the Social Media Victims Law Center and Temple University's Institute for Law, Innovation & Technology filed it jointly.

He says he had symptoms of a pulmonary embolism for six weeks that the chatbot attributed to something else.

His case matters beyond his own losses, because hundreds of millions of people put health questions to the same product every week — and most of them have never checked what protections come with the answers.

What the filing describes — and what it cost Winters

Winters had been unwell since 2022 and was diagnosed with small intestinal bacterial overgrowth and chronic prostatitis in 2024. He began using ChatGPT in June 2024 for ordinary questions about geography, politics and biblical texts. He used the free version of ChatGPT for most of the period described in the complaint. He bought a paid ChatGPT Plus subscription in June 2025, the month before his collapse.

By March 2025 the chatbot was writing him supplement dosing schedules. By June it had diagnosed him with dysautonomia and told him to stay "recliner-bound."

In late June it told him he was "nowhere near the count or depth" that leads to long-term bedbound scenarios, and that his recliner-based recovery was buying his future. His lawyers read that exchange as setting a threshold of eight to 10 more episodes before his condition would count as serious. He stayed off his feet.

content_html_zh:
戈德温·奥卢庞米尔

2026 年 7 月 24 日星期五上午 8:30 CDT 阅读时间 7 分钟

2025 年 6 月 8 日，斯科特·温特斯 (Scott Winters) 正在讲道，房间开始旋转，情况严重到他不得不停止礼拜。 教堂工作人员打电话给护理人员，医护人员对他进行了检查，并表示他看起来情况稳定。

当他回到家时，他打开 ChatGPT 来解释这一事件。 根据 7 月 22 日代表他向旧金山县高等法院提交的诉状，ChatGPT 告诉他“放轻松”，并表示他的症状会随着时间的推移而改善。

必读

  • 杰夫·贝索斯 (Jeff Bezos) 支持一个平台，让任何人都可以以低至 100 美元的价格投资出租房屋——无需真正成为房东即可像房东一样积累财富的 6 种方法
  • Dave Ramsey 警告说，近 50% 的美国人在社会保障方面犯了 1 个重大错误——这就是它的本质，以及尽快修复它的 3 个简单步骤
  • 43岁以下的百万富翁仅将其财富的25%投资于股票。 这就是他们的钱实际去向的地方

不幸的是，2025年7月13日，他因双肺出现血栓而被送往重症监护室。

55 岁的 Winters 是一位居住在佛罗里达州的前牧师和房地产专业人士，他正在起诉 OpenAI 和首席执行官 Sam Altman，指控其八项诉讼理由，包括严格的产品责任、疏忽和违反加州反不正当竞争法。 科技司法法、社交媒体受害者法律中心和天普大学法律、创新与技术研究所联合提起诉讼。

他说，他有六周的肺栓塞症状，聊天机器人将其归因于其他原因。

他的案件比他自己的损失更重要，因为每周都有数亿人对同一种产品提出健康问题，而他们中的大多数人从未检查过答案附带哪些保护措施。

文件描述了什么——以及温特斯付出的代价

温特斯自2022年以来一直身体不适，并于2024年被诊断出患有小肠细菌过度生长和慢性前列腺炎。 他于 2024 年 6 月开始使用 ChatGPT 来回答有关地理、政治和圣经文本的普通问题。 在投诉中描述的大部分时间里，他都使用免费版本的 ChatGPT。 他于 2025 年 6 月（也就是他崩溃的前一个月）购买了 ChatGPT Plus 付费订阅。

到 2025 年 3 月，聊天机器人正在为他编写补充剂量时间表。 到了六月，它诊断出他患有自主神经功能障碍，并告诉他要“躺在躺椅上”。

六月下旬，它告诉他，他“远没有达到导致长期卧床不起的情况”，而他基于躺椅的康复正在购买他的未来。 他的律师认为，这次交流设置了一个门槛，即他的病情还要再发生八到十次才算严重。 他站了起来。
